skill.name: hook-development
skill.description: Guidance and utilities for building Claude Code hooks that validate tool use, enforce safety rules, load project context, and test hook configurations.
skill.summary: Build Claude Code hooks for validation, automation, testing, and policy enforcement.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, secret, and blocker patterns, but most are examples that teach validation or test denial behavior. The package is not malicious and can publish with warnings, but command-hook guidance and the test helper create a real local shell-execution risk if users pass untrusted paths or copy examples without review.

medium_findings.title: Shell command composition in hook test helper
medium_findings.description: The test helper builds a bash -c command from positional arguments. This is useful for local testing, but untrusted hook script paths or input paths could alter the shell command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly interpolates argument-derived values into a bash -c string. The context is a local test utility, so risk depends on who controls those arguments.

medium_findings.title: Command hook patterns execute local shell scripts
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches command hooks that run bash scripts through Claude Code events. This is expected for hook development, but users must review generated hooks because they can read files, inspect environment data, and block workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented examples intentionally execute bash for hook validation. This is a legitimate capability, not malicious behavior, but it expands the execution surface.

low_findings.title: Destructive command strings are defensive examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged disk and recursive delete strings. The reviewed locations use those strings to deny dangerous commands or to test that validators block them, not to execute destructive operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code compares command text and emits deny decisions, or supplies dangerous text as test input. I found no evidence that these snippets execute disk destruction.

low_findings.title: Sensitive file and secret references are validation criteria
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged environment files, credential terms, and secret patterns. The reviewed examples use these references to warn, deny, or detect sensitive writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is protective validation of paths and content. I found no evidence of credential collection, exfiltration, or unauthorized secret access.

low_findings.title: No prompt injection attempt found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find instructions that ask the evaluator or runtime model to ignore prior instructions, skip review, or claim special approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I searched the skill files for common prompt-injection markers and reviewed the overview context. No evidence found, though this does not prove every phrase is harmless.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell command composition in hook test helper
dangerous_patterns.description: The test helper builds a bash -c command from positional arguments. This is useful for local testing, but untrusted hook script paths or input paths could alter the shell command.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script directly interpolates argument-derived values into a bash -c string. The context is a local test utility, so risk depends on who controls those arguments.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command hook patterns execute local shell scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill teaches command hooks that run bash scripts through Claude Code events. This is expected for hook development, but users must review generated hooks because they can read files, inspect environment data, and block workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented examples intentionally execute bash for hook validation. This is a legitimate capability, not malicious behavior, but it expands the execution surface.

content.value_statement: Claude Code hooks can be hard to design safely because they run during tool events. This skill gives patterns, examples, and scripts for validation, testing, and policy enforcement.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code hook events including PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionStart, and Notification.
Shows prompt-based hooks for contextual validation and command hooks for deterministic checks.
Provides Bash examples for command validation, write validation, and project context loading.
Includes utility scripts to validate hook schemas, lint hook scripts, and test hooks with sample input.
Documents migration from basic command hooks to prompt-based hook configurations.
Covers debugging, restart behavior, matcher syntax, timeouts, and hook output formats.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples; it does not install production hooks automatically.
Command hooks still require careful shell review before use in shared environments.
Prompt hook behavior depends on supported Claude Code events and model judgment.
Examples need adaptation for each project policy, operating system, and plugin layout.

use_cases.title: Add tool-use guardrails
use_cases.description: Create hooks that review Bash, Write, and Edit actions before they run.

use_cases.title: Standardize team policies
use_cases.description: Document and test rules for sensitive files, destructive commands, and completion checks.

use_cases.title: Migrate to prompt hooks
use_cases.description: Replace brittle shell-only checks with prompt-based validation where context matters.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic validation hook
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one tool event.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Claude Code PreToolUse hook that asks before writing to sensitive files. Explain each field briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a script hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my Bash validation hook into a prompt-based hook. Preserve the same safety policy and list any behavior changes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a plugin hook set
prompt_templates.prompt: Design hooks for a plugin that blocks dangerous Bash commands, checks tests before Stop, and loads project context at SessionStart.

prompt_templates.title: Audit hook implementation risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Claude Code hooks for command injection, path traversal, secret exposure, timeout risk, and unclear denial behavior.

output_examples.input: A request to add a hook that blocks destructive Bash commands.
output_examples.output: A PreToolUse hook plan with matcher choice, denial criteria, timeout guidance, and test cases for safe and unsafe commands.

output_examples.input: A request to migrate hard-coded path checks to prompt hooks.
output_examples.output: A prompt-based validation approach that checks system paths, credential files, traversal, symlinks, and content sensitivity.

output_examples.input: A request to validate a hook package before release.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering schema validation, script linting, sample inputs, debug testing, restart behavior, and user-facing error messages.

best_practices: Prefer prompt hooks for contextual safety decisions and command hooks for fast deterministic checks.
Quote shell variables, validate hook input, set timeouts, and test hooks before deployment.
Use plugin-relative paths and document each hook decision so users understand denials.
anti_patterns: Do not pass untrusted paths or commands into shell strings without strict quoting and validation.
Do not rely on hook execution order when hooks may run in parallel.
Do not deploy hooks that silently fail, run for too long, or block work without clear messages.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps build Claude Code hooks for validation, automation, context loading, and workflow policy checks.

faq.question: Does it support prompt-based hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends prompt hooks for context-aware decisions on supported events.

faq.question: Can it create command hooks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes command hook examples and scripts, but they require careful shell security review.

faq.question: Are the included examples production ready?
faq.answer: They are starting points. Review paths, matchers, messages, and policies before production use.

faq.question: How should I test hooks?
faq.answer: Use the included schema validator, hook linter, test helper, and Claude Code debug mode.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It teaches shell-executing hooks and includes a local test helper that composes shell commands from arguments.
